attributes.stem:
A three-year-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with his parent with his fourth episode of pneumonia in the past year. He has had six episodes of otitis media, three episodes of viral gastroenteritis, and frequent upper respiratory tract infections since starting childcare at age one. He was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery. His immunisations are up to date. He has no other significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears well. His vital signs are within normal limits. Chest examination reveals scattered crackles in the right lower zone. Laboratory investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin: 125 g/L (110-140)
  • White cell count: 7.2 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0)
  • Neutrophil count: 4.2 x 10^9/L (1.5-8.5)
  • Lymphocyte count: 1.8 x 10^9/L (1.0-4.0)
  • Platelet count: 280 x 10^9/L (150-400)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step for the evaluation?
attributes.explanation:
This child presents with recurrent sinopulmonary infections and viral illnesses suggestive of primary immunodeficiency. The pattern of infections, particularly the frequency of pneumonia, otitis media, and viral infections, raises concern for an antibody deficiency disorder.

Serum immunoglobulin levels are the most appropriate first-line investigation as they are widely available, cost-effective, and can identify the presence and pattern of antibody deficiency. Low levels of IgG, with variable IgA and IgM levels, would suggest conditions such as common variable immunodeficiency or X-linked agammaglobulinaemia.

Serum protein electrophoresis is primarily used to detect monoclonal proteins in conditions like multiple myeloma that can cause secondary hypogammaglobulinaemia. This would be extremely unlikely in a three-year-old child and would not be an appropriate first-line test.

Genetic testing may be useful in identifying specific mutations associated with primary immunodeficiencies but should be guided by initial laboratory findings. It is expensive, time-consuming, and should not be the first-line investigation.

Pneumococcal vaccine response assessment is useful in confirming antibody deficiency but should be performed after initial immunoglobulin levels are known. It helps assess the functional capacity of antibody production.

Neutrophil oxidative burst test is used to diagnose chronic granulomatous disease, which typically presents with infections caused by catalase-positive organisms such as Staphylococcus aureus, Burkholderia cepacia, Serratia marcescens, and fungi like Aspergillus species. The presence of viral infections in this case makes chronic granulomatous disease less likely, as patients with this condition typically have normal responses to viral pathogens.

After measuring immunoglobulin levels, further testing may include specific antibody responses, lymphocyte subsets, and genetic testing depending on the pattern of abnormalities found. Treatment will depend on the specific immunodeficiency identified but may include immunoglobulin replacement therapy and appropriate antimicrobial prophylaxis.
learningPoints: Primary immunodeficiency should be suspected in children with recurrent sinopulmonary infections.
Measurement of immunoglobulin levels is the first-line investigation for suspected antibody deficiency.
references: McCusker C, Upton J, Warrington R. Primary immunodeficiency. Allergy Asthma Clin Immunol. 2018;14(Suppl 2):61. Published 2018 Sep 12. doi:10.1186/s13223-018-0290-5
Lee AY, Gray PE. Evaluating for immunodeficiency in children with recurrent infection. Aust Fam Physician. 2014;43(9):629-632.
